title: Scarcity, choice and opportunity cost
paperName: Cambridge International AS Level Economics (P1 & P2)
summary: 9708 AS foundational economics — scarcity, finite resources, opportunity cost, and the basic economic problem.
learningObjectives: Define scarcity and explain why it applies to all economies.
Explain opportunity cost as the next best alternative forgone.
Apply opportunity cost to individual, firm, and government decisions.
Link scarcity to the three basic economic questions.

simpleExplanation.title: Unlimited wants, limited means
simpleExplanation.summary: Scarcity means resources are finite while wants are unlimited. Every choice has an opportunity cost — the best alternative you give up.
steps: Scarcity forces choice — not all wants can be satisfied.
Opportunity cost is not just money — it is the forgone alternative.
What to produce? How? For whom? — answered differently in market vs planned economies.
PPC diagrams (1.5) visualise opportunity cost as slope.

sections.content: **1.1** is the conceptual foundation for all 9708 micro and macro. Cambridge essay marks reward **precise definitions** and **applied examples**, not vague generalities.

items: Scarce ≠ short supply — scarcity is relative to unlimited wants
Free goods have zero opportunity cost (no alternative forgone)
Government spending has OC — e.g. defence vs healthcare
Rational decision at margin: compare extra benefit vs extra cost

sections.question: A student chooses to study economics for an hour instead of working a part-time job paying £8. What is the opportunity cost?
sections.solution:
The **£8 wage** (or whatever could have been earned) — the next best alternative forgone.

If leisure was valued more than £8, the OC of studying might be that leisure.

sections.content: Always state opportunity cost as **the specific alternative forgone**, not 'time' or 'money' alone. Examiners reject vague OC definitions.
